title: NAT Pool Exhaustion
spl:
index=firewall ("NAT" OR "nat") ("exhausted" OR "allocation failed" OR "out of")
| stats count by host, nat_pool | sort -count
cimSpl:
| tstats `summariesonly` count sum(All_Traffic.bytes_in) as bytes_in sum(All_Traffic.bytes_out) as bytes_out
  from datamodel=Network_Traffic.All_Traffic
  by All_Traffic.src All_Traffic.dest All_Traffic.action span=1h
| eval bytes=bytes_in+bytes_out
| sort -bytes
description: NAT exhaustion prevents outbound connections. Users lose internet access.
value: NAT exhaustion prevents outbound connections. Users lose internet access.
implementation: Forward firewall logs. Monitor NAT table usage. Alert on exhaustion messages or >80% utilization.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
‚ÄĘ Install and configure the required add-on or app: `Splunk_TA_paloalto`, `TA-fortinet_fortigate`, Cisco Secure Firewall Add-on, `Splunk_TA_juniper` (SRX), syslog.
‚ÄĘ Ensure the following data sources are available: Firewall NAT/system logs.
‚ÄĘ For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 ‚ÄĒ Configure data collection
Forward firewall logs. Monitor NAT table usage. Alert on exhaustion messages or >80% utilization.

Step 2 ‚ÄĒ Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
index=firewall ("NAT" OR "nat") ("exhausted" OR "allocation failed" OR "out of")
| stats count by host, nat_pool | sort -count
```

Understanding this SPL

**NAT Pool Exhaustion** ‚ÄĒ NAT exhaustion prevents outbound connections. Users lose internet access.

Documented **Data sources**: Firewall NAT/system logs. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): `Splunk_TA_paloalto`, `TA-fortinet_fortigate`, Cisco Secure Firewall Add-on, `Splunk_TA_juniper` (SRX), syslog. The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed‚ÄĒrename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

The first pipeline stage scopes events using **index**: firewall.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

‚ÄĘ Scopes the data: index=firewall. Cross-check against **Data sources** above so indexes and sourcetypes match your ingestion.
‚ÄĘ `stats` rolls up events into metrics; results are split **by host, nat_pool** so each row reflects one combination of those dimensions (useful for per-host, per-user, or per-entity comparisons for this use case).
‚ÄĘ Orders rows with `sort` ‚ÄĒ combine with `head`/`tail` for top-N patterns.

Optional CIM / accelerated variant (same use case, normalized fields via Common Information Model):

```spl
| tstats `summariesonly` count sum(All_Traffic.bytes_in) as bytes_in sum(All_Traffic.bytes_out) as bytes_out
  from datamodel=Network_Traffic.All_Traffic
  by All_Traffic.src All_Traffic.dest All_Traffic.action span=1h
| eval bytes=bytes_in+bytes_out
| sort -bytes
```

Understanding this CIM / accelerated SPL

**NAT Pool Exhaustion** ‚ÄĒ NAT exhaustion prevents outbound connections. Users lose internet access.

Documented **Data sources**: Firewall NAT/system logs. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): `Splunk_TA_paloalto`, `TA-fortinet_fortigate`, Cisco Secure Firewall Add-on, `Splunk_TA_juniper` (SRX), syslog. The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed‚ÄĒrename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

This **CIM or accelerated** block uses normalized field names and/or `tstats` over data models. Enable **acceleration** on the referenced models (and correct CIM knowledge objects) or the search may return nothing.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

‚ÄĘ Uses `tstats` against accelerated summaries for data model `Network_Traffic.All_Traffic` ‚ÄĒ enable acceleration for that model.
‚ÄĘ `eval` defines or adjusts **bytes** ‚ÄĒ often to normalize units, derive a ratio, or prepare for thresholds.
‚ÄĘ Orders rows with `sort` ‚ÄĒ combine with `head`/`tail` for top-N patterns.

Enable Data Model Acceleration (and metric indexes for `mstats`) for the models or datasets referenced above; otherwise `tstats`/`mstats` may return no results from summaries.


Step 3 ‚ÄĒ Validate
Confirm that events are present in the index and that the search returns expected results. Compare with known good/bad scenarios if applicable. Verify field extractions and index permissions.

Step 4 ‚ÄĒ Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Gauge per pool, Table, Events timeline.
visualization: Gauge per pool, Table, Events timeline.
hardware: Cisco Secure Firewall 3110/3120/3130/3140, Firepower 1010/1120/1140/1150, Firepower 2110/2120/2130/2140, FMC; Palo Alto PA-220/PA-440/PA-450/PA-460/PA-3200/PA-5200/PA-5400/PA-7000, Panorama; Fortinet FortiGate 40F/60F/80F/100F/200F/400F/600F/1000F/1800F/2600F/3500F/3700F/4400F, FortiManager; Juniper SRX300/SRX320/SRX340/SRX345/SRX550/SRX1500/SRX4100/SRX4200/SRX4600/SRX5400/SRX5600/SRX5800 (FMC)
